coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-integrated portable platform based on polychromatic ratiometric fluorescent paper sensors for visual quantitative determination of norfloxacin
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jiaheng
Wang, Jun
Ouyang, Feijun
Zheng, Zhi
Huang, Xiaowan
Zhang, Haitao
He, Dongxiu
He, Suisui
Wei, Hua
Yu, Cui-Yun
coredata.dc:description: The emergence of “superbugs” due to antibiotics overuse poses a significant threat to human health and security. The development of sensitive and effective antibiotics detection is undoubtedly a prerequisite for addressing antibiotics overuse-associated issues. However, current techniques for monitoring antibiotics typically require costly equipment and well-trained professionals. Hence, we developed herein a rapid, instrument-free, and on-site detection method for antibiotic residues such as norfloxacin (NOR) based on a ratiometric sensing platform utilizing “on–off–on” response properties of polychromatic fluorescence for direct visual quantitative NOR analysis. Specifically, this platform integrated iron ions (Fe3+)-chelated blue carbon dots (BCDs) for signal sensing and red carbon dots (RCDs) as an internal reference. The sensor mechanism is selective quenching of BCDs' blue fluorescence by Fe3+ via an inner filter effect with unaffected RCDs' red fluorescence. Further NOR addition led to competitive binding with BCDs due to Fe3+ shedding from the BCDs’ surface for a recovered blue fluorescence signal. Notably, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor demonstrated rapid and highly sensitive NOR detection in a concentration range of 1–70 μM with an impressive detection limit of 6.84 nM. The ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform was constructed by integrating smartphone and paper-based strategies, which exhibited exceptional sensitivity, selectivity, and rapid response for portable, instrument-free, visual quantification of NOR in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Smartphone platform
Visual detection
Norfloxacin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of the dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Construction of a smartphone sensing platform
Analysis of NOR in real samples
The detection principle of NOR and construction of the portable smartphone sensing platform
Characterization of the BCDs and RCDs
Sensitive and selective detection of NOR
Smartphone sensing platform for rapid NOR tracking
Detection of NOR in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) BCDs and (b) RCDs, and (c) their size distribution. XPS spectra of (d) C 1s, (e) N 1s, and (f) O 1s in BCDs, (g) C 1s and (h) N 1s in RCDs. (i) Emission spectrum of BCDs, RCDs, and B/RCDs, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of B/RCDs@Fe3+ with increasing NOR concentrations (0–70 μM) upon excitation at 360 nm and (b) the relationship between I440/I620 and NOR concentration diagram (the inset image showed the corresponding fluorescence picture captured under a 365 nm UV lamp). (c) CIE chromaticity diagram of B/RCDs@Fe3+ to NOR and (d) the corresponding linear fitting diagram.

(a,b) Selectivity and (c,d) anti-interference test to NOR of interfering substances (Arg, ASP, Tyr, Ca2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Br−, Cl−, SO4 2−, CIP, and SD). Corresponding fluorescence images captured under a 365 nm UV lamp.

Illustration of the smartphone-based portable sensing platform. (b) The relationship of B/R values of the paper strips with NOR concentration. Inset showed the fluorescence images with increasing NOR concentrations (0–80 μM) under a 365 nm UV light.

Schematic illustration of the synthetic route of (a) BCDs and (b) RCDs, as well as (c) the visual detection principle for NOR by integrating the dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescent probe and a smartphone sensing platform.
Determination and recovery test of NOR spiked in real sample.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-integrated portable platform based on polychromatic ratiometric fluorescent paper sensors for visual quantitative determination of norfloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Province Cooperative Innovation Center for Molecular Target New Drug Study, School of Pharmaceutical Science, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang, 421001, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Province Cooperative Innovation Center for Molecular Target New Drug Study

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Province Cooperative Innovation Center for Molecular Target New Drug Study, School of Pharmaceutical Science, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang, 421001, Hunan, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emergence of “superbugs” due to antibiotics overuse poses a significant threat to human health and security. The development of sensitive and effective antibiotics detection is undoubtedly a prerequisite for addressing antibiotics overuse-associated issues. However, current techniques for monitoring antibiotics typically require costly equipment and well-trained professionals. Hence, we developed herein a rapid, instrument-free, and on-site detection method for antibiotic residues such as norfloxacin (NOR) based on a ratiometric sensing platform utilizing “on–off–on” response properties of polychromatic fluorescence for direct visual quantitative NOR analysis. Specifically, this platform integrated iron ions (Fe3+)-chelated blue carbon dots (BCDs) for signal sensing and red carbon dots (RCDs) as an internal reference. The sensor mechanism is selective quenching of BCDs' blue fluorescence by Fe3+ via an inner filter effect with unaffected RCDs' red fluorescence. Further NOR addition led to competitive binding with BCDs due to Fe3+ shedding from the BCDs’ surface for a recovered blue fluorescence signal. Notably, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor demonstrated rapid and highly sensitive NOR detection in a concentration range of 1–70 μM with an impressive detection limit of 6.84 nM. The ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform was constructed by integrating smartphone and paper-based strategies, which exhibited exceptional sensitivity, selectivity, and rapid response for portable, instrument-free, visual quantification of NOR in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceutical drugs such as antibiotics are indispensable for maintaining the health of both animals and humans that have been extensively utilized in disease prevention and treatment [1–3]. However, antibiotic residues and their metabolites, which present potential risks of antimicrobial resistance, anaphylactic reactions, and cancer inducement owing to their undesirable persistence and biological activity in the natural environment, have garnered global attention [4,5]. Norfloxacin (NOR), a third-generation quinolone antibiotic, has been extensively used for clinical treatment of respiratory, urinary, and gastrointestinal infectious diseases caused by susceptible bacteria in both human and animal populations, which inevitably led to significant NOR accumulation in the environment and posed a great threat to both ecosystems and human health [6]. Therefore, development of sensitive and effective method for NOR detection in foods and environmental samples is undoubtedly a prerequisite for addressing NOR overuse-associated issues.

Currently, the detection of NOR primarily relies on conventional technical protocols such as high-performance liquid chromatography [7], enzyme immunoassay [8], and capillary electrophoresis [9]. These methods generally suffered from expensive apparatus, time-consuming sample operations, and requirements for professional expertise despite of the accurate NOR quantification properties. Some emerging strategies have been developed recently for NOR sensing, including electrochemical, colorimetric, and fluorescent methods [10–12]. Fluorescent detection is a vigoroso analytical method owing to its straightforward operation, high accuracy and sensitivity [13–18]. Various fluorescent strategies have been developed for NOR sensing based on the interactions between NOR and fluorescent sensors including mental organic framework [19], covalent organic frameworks [20], perovskite nanocrystals [21], copper nanoclusters [22], quantum dots [23], and lanthanide ions [24]. The majority of developed fluorescent methods rely on single-wavelength fluorescence intensity, either with turn-on or turn-off signal modulation, which is susceptible to ambient interference, apparatus deviations, and excitation light sources for possible incorrect or unreliable results. A ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform containing self-calibration capability offers an alternative means to overcome these drawbacks and to reduce disturbance while improving accuracy [25–27]. Additionally, the ratiometric assay that enables distinguishable color changes is more expedient for visual sensing applications [28–30]. As an extremely promising handheld and point-of-care testing platform, smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescence detection has been utilized for on-site determination of hazardous materials [31–33]. With these consideration in mind, there is an urgent imperative to exploit intelligent visual instruments that can perform accurate on-site/real-time sensing of NOR in a portable and inexpensive manner to conquer the aforementioned deficiencies.

Sub-10 nm carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention due to the notable advantages of adjustable fluorescence characteristics, hypotoxicity, excellent dispersion, cost-effective manufacturing, and facile surface functionalization [34–37]. These desirable properties propel the widespread use of CDs in various fields, especially in fluorescence sensing [38,39]. Numerous studies have reported on the applications of fluorescent sensors based on CDs in the quantitative detection of heavy metal ions and toxic organic pollutants, but there have been relatively few studies reporting the use of these properties on antibiotics detection so far [40–43]. Moreover, CDs have been always adopted as a single fluorescence probe for detection, which is easily interfered by background signals and environment factors [44,45]. A ratiometric fluorescence of CDs can overcome the restrictions to improve the signal-to-noise ratio due to the ability to self-calibrate detection signals [46–48]. To our knowledge, the CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensors for the visual quantitative detection of NOR is still undeveloped.

For this purpose, we designed and developed a novel portable smartphone sensor platform for rapid, convenient, and visual quantitative detection of NOR, whose operation is conducted by a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor incorporating blue carbon dots (BCDs) and red carbon dots (RCDs) via an “on–off–on” response mechanism. This platform integrated iron ions (Fe3+)-chelated BCDs for signal sensing and RCDs as an internal reference. Further NOR addition resulted in competitive binding with BCDs because of Fe3+ shedding from the BCDs’ surface for a recovered blue fluorescence signal. The fluorescence color of the dual-emission ratiometric nanosensor transitioned gradually from pink to purple to blue (Scheme 1 ). Finally, a portable sensor platform with exceptional stability and reliability was developed by integrating a smartphone and test papers loaded with the nanosensors, which demonstrates great potential for practical applications as a convenient means for on-site and real-time detection of NOR in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: BCDs and RCDs were prepared via hydrothermal and solvothermal methods according to the previous literature, respectively [49]. The two types of carbon dots were mixed in appropriate proportions and diluted to 1 mL with pure water. Fluorescence spectra were recorded under different conditions, including probe mixing ratio, buffer solution, pH value, incubation time, and excitation wavelength. The changes of the fluorescence intensity ratios (I440/I620) were calculated accordingly. Subsequently, an Fe3+-quenched dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe was constructed under optimal conditions.

Under optimal conditions, a mixed solution of BCDs (0.4 mg/mL), RCDs (0.3 mg/mL) and FeCl3 (0.4 μM) was prepared by diluting them to 1 mL with pure water in a centrifugal tube. After adding NOR with different concentrations, the emission spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer following incubation for 3 min. The influence and selectivity of coexisting ions on NOR were evaluated using l-arginine (Arg), tyrosine (Tyr), l-aspartic acid (ASP), Ca2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Br−, Cl−, SO4 2−, ciprofloxacin (CIP), and sulfamethoxazole (SD). The NOR concentration was fixed at 4 μM, while the concentrations of the other possible interfering analytes were set at a value of 40 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a smartphone sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The double-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe was prepared and loaded on Whatman filter paper. After incubation for 30 s, the test paper was transferred to an oven at 60 °C for drying. Finally, a smartphone sensor platform was constructed by integrating high-definition camera and color recognizer application software for visual detection of NOR. The fluorescence image on the strip was captured by the high-definition camera of the smartphone under ultraviolet lamp excitation, and then converted into red, green and blue (RGB) values by internal color recognition application software. Finally, NOR concentration was quantified through calculating the ratio of blue and red channels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of NOR in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the portable smartphone sensor platform for detecting NOR was assessed using pure water, tap water, local Xiangjiang River water, and purchased milk as test samples. Tap water was sourced from the laboratory, whereas the Xiangjiang sample was directly collected from the river. A bottled water and milk were purchased from a local supermarket. In addition to diluting the milk 10 times for testing purposes, other samples were subjected to direct analysis without any pretreatment. Based on the standard addition method, different concentrations of NOR were added to the aforementioned samples. The samples were then dripped onto test paper and data was recorded using the Color Recognizer on a smartphone. The mean value of the data was calculated by four independent experiments and the standard deviation was determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection principle of NOR and construction of the portable smartphone sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescent probe (B/RCDs@Fe3+) that composed of BCDs, RCDs, and Fe3+ was used for the visual determination of NOR. As illustrated in Scheme 1a and 1b, the bright blue BCDs and pink RCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal and solvothermal methods, respectively. The visual detection mechanism of NOR is based on the as-prepared B/RCDs@Fe3+, in which BCDs can serve as a signal unit and RCDs act as an internal standard on account of the distinction in surface functional groups. With the addition of Fe3+, the blue fluorescence of BCDs was quenched by the chelation of Fe3+ and carboxyl groups on the BCDs surface, which ascribed to the inner filter effect (IFE) based on a considerable overlap between the absorption spectra of Fe3+ and the excitation spectrum of BCDs. Meanwhile, the red emission of RCDs had no significant change owing to the good stability between Fe3+ and the surface amino. The different responses of those two carbon dots resulted in the color change of luminescence. The introduction of NOR gradually recovered the blue fluorescence, which could be reasonably attributed to the stronger affinity between Fe3+ and NOR for subsequent competitive binding and cleavage of original coordination bonding between Fe3+ and carboxyl groups of BCDs. In order to achieve portable, sensitive and instrument-free determination, a paper-based smartphone sensing platform was developed for on-site/real-time analysis of target (Scheme 1c). Under the excitation of UV light, different fluorescent colors in the paper strip can be read out through the high-definition camera of a smartphone. The Color Recognition APP installed on a smartphone was utilized to analyze fluorescence images and derived corresponding RGB values by fitting. The RGB values can be employed for quantitative analysis of the target object, thereby enabling on-site/real-time detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the BCDs and RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The BCDs and RCDs were characterized using a range of analytical techniques, including transmission electron microscope (TEM), UV–vis absorption, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), and fluorescence spectrum. As depicted in Fig. 1 a–c, the synthesized BCDs and RCDs exhibited excellent monodispersity with average sizes of 2.28 ± 0.07 nm and 4.13 ± 0.01 nm, respectively. FT-IR spectra of BCDs displayed the characteristic absorption peaks at wavenumbers of 3417 cm−1 (N–H/O–H stretching vibration), 1605 cm−1 (COO− stretching vibration or N–H bending vibration), 1403 cm−1 (C–N stretching vibration), and 1072 cm−1 (C–O stretching vibration) (Fig. S1a). The absorption band of RCDs at 3374–3200, 1630, 1516, 1263, and 831 cm−1 were assigned to N–H or O–H stretching vibration, CC stretching vibration, C–N stretching vibration, C–O stretching vibration, and the plane bending of the benzene ring, respectively (Fig. S1b). Additionally, XPS data were conducted to confirm the surface element and valence states of the BCDs and RCDs (Fig. S2). In the XPS spectra of BCDs, the C 1s band was deconvoluted into three peaks that the binding energies at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.7 eV were assigned to CC, C–N, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 1d). A peak at 399.6 eV was attributed to N 1s corresponding to C–N (Fig. 1e). In O 1s spectra, the peaks of 530.8, 531.9, and 533.3 eV corresponded to C–O, C–OH, and CO, respectively (Fig. 1f). For RCDs, the C 1s band was resolved into three peaks at 284.3, 285.2, and 286.0 eV, corresponding to CC, C–N, and C–O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1g). The peaks observed at 398.4, 399.1, and 400.0 eV correspond to pyridine N, amino N, and pyrrole N, respectively (Fig. 1h). These results indicated that the successful synthesis of the BCDs and RCDs with carboxyl and amino groups in the surface of BCDs and only amino groups in RCDs.

In addition, the optical properties of the BCDs and RCDs have been investigated, revealing a characteristic absorption at 334 and 443 nm, respectively (Fig. S3). Under photoexcitation at 360 nm, the fluorescence spectra exhibit a bright blue fluorescence at 440 nm in BCDs and a pink fluorescence at 620 nm in RCDs (Fig. 1i). By combining BCDs and RCDs, the dual-emissive carbon dots (B/RCDs) exhibited two distinct emission peaks that match well with those of the individual BCDs and RCDs.

To establish the B/RCDs@Fe3+ based on fluorescence “on−off−on” response, Fe3+ was chosen as a fluorescent quencher considering its unique response mechanism. The quencher concentrations in B/RCDs@Fe3+ were determined by the variation tendency of the fluorescent systems upon Fe3+ addition. The decrease in emission intensity at 440 nm with increasing Fe3+ concentration was evident, while the fluorescence at 620 nm remained unchanged (Fig. S4a). The fluorescence quenching rate gradually slowed down when the Fe3+ concentration reached 0.4 μM. Therefore, the optimal Fe3+ concentration was determined to be 0.4 μM with a quenching efficiency reaching approximately 61.8% (Figs. S4b and 4c). TEM images showed that BCDs aggregated after adding Fe3+, while RCDs maintained the original dispersion status (Fig. S5). The zeta potential of BCDs increased from −7.39 mV to −3.01 mV that the consumption of negative charges was caused by the chelation of positively charged Fe3+ with the carboxyl group on BCDs, while the surface charge of RCDs remained nearly constant (Fig. S6). These results were consistent with the fluorescence spectra, indicating the successful construction of the dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescence probe. To elucidate the fluorescence quenching principle, the excitation spectra of BCDs and the absorption pattern of Fe3+ in the sensor system were compared. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+ exhibited a strong characteristic absorption peak at 291 nm, which overlapped greatly with the excitation spectra of BCDs (the maximum excitation wavelength at 341 nm) (Fig. S7a). In addition, there was no significant change in the fluorescence lifetime of BCDs before and after Fe3+ treatment, corresponding to 6.57 and 6.53 ns, respectively (Fig. S7b). These results suggested that the quenching of the blue fluorescence in BCDs can be attributed to the IFE.

In order to obtain the optimal detection conditions, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I620)/(I440/I620)0 of the B/RCDs@Fe3+ with different concentration ratios of BCDs and RCDs (1:3∼6:3) to Fe3+ quenching was investigated. As shown in Fig. S8a, Fe3+ quenching effect was optimal when the CBCDs/CRCDs value was 4:3. After a comprehensive evaluation of the fluorescence intensity and visualization performance, the reaction conditions (Tris buffer solution, pH = 7.4), reaction time (3 min), and excitation wavelength (360 nm) were investigated and optimized on the B/RCDs@Fe3+ (Figs. S8b–8e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of NOR
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the detection performance of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor, we investigated the response of B/RCDs@Fe3+ to NOR in pure water under optimal conditions. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the fluorescence intensities of BCDs recorded at 440 nm showed a gradual increase tendency with the increasing NOR concentrations, while the emission intensities of RCDs acquired at 620 nm remained relatively stable. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I620) of B/RCDs@Fe3+ showed a typical ratiometric response and significant fluorescence color changes from pink to purple, and finally to blue. By plotting the I440/I620 versus the NOR concentrations, the fitted linear equation was y = 3.78112–0.65472x (R2 = 0.99991) in the range of 0–70 μM; the limit of detection (LOD) was as low as 6.84 nM by using the Hubaux and Vos method [50,51] (Fig. 2b). Accordingly, this result was further confirmed by the theoretical CIE coordinate analysis, which facilitated the development of sensitive visual analysis for NOR based on ratiometric signals (Fig. 2c and d).

Since selectivity is also considered as a key parameter of detection performance, we further evaluated the selectivity recognition ability of the ratiometric fluorescent probes for NOR sensing. By comparing fluorescence intensity and fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I620)/(I440/I620)0, the response capacity of B/RCDs@Fe3+ to NOR was estimated when introducing 10-fold concentrations of other substrates such as Arg, ASP, Tyr, Ca2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Br−, Cl−, SO4 2−, CIP, and SD. The results indicated that only NOR could lead to a significantly enhanced (I440/I620)/(I440/I620)0 value, while the other substrates could not elicit any response (Fig. 3 a and b). In addition, the interference experiment results revealed that other interfering substances had insignificant effect on detection signals in the coexistence of NOR (Fig. 3c and d). These facts strongly suggested that the higher selectivity of B/RCDs@Fe3+ than these interfering ions could meet the requirements of selective NOR analysis in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone sensing platform for rapid NOR tracking
ce:section.ce:para: Although the ratiometric sensor-based paper strips enabled fluorescence visual determination, achieving satisfactory sensitivity and accuracy was challenging due to the limitations of naked-eye evaluation in distinguishing subtle color changes. In order to improve the reliability of analysis outcomes and the portability of the operation, it was imperative to employ a visual, quantitative, and instrument-free sensing approach for on-site determination. For this purpose, smartphone was developed for converting color information to RGB values by its high-definition camera and apps. To mitigate the interference of extraneous UV rays, an optical filter with a cut-off wavelength of >400 nm was inserted between the sample slot and light source to effectively eliminate undesired excitation light (Fig. 4 a). Under the excitation of UV lamp, the fluorescence image on the strip was captured by the high-definition camera of the smartphone, and the corresponding image color was subsequently converted into RGB values by the internal color recognizer application. Finally, the concentration of NOR was quantized by calculating the ratio of blue and red channels (B/R). As shown in Fig. 4b, B/R values increased with NOR concentration increasing, which showed a good linear relationship with NOR concentration (B/R = 0.01833 + 0.01340 [NOR], R2 = 0.99118) in the range of 0–80 μM, and the LOD was 7.13 μM. The results indicated that the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform achieved a portable, visual and quantitative detection of NOR without any complex instruments. In addition, we monitored the stability of the paper strips, which was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The B/R values of the fluorescent strip remained almost unchanged within 28 days, indicating that it had high stability (Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NOR in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: As NOR residues are primarily present in environmental water systems, we selected ultrapure water, tap water, Xiangjiang River water, and milk to validate the practicability of the sensing platform for the NOR determination of actual samples. After NOR addition at various concentrations of 10, 20, and 40 nM, the spike and recovery study were conducted using the portable sensing platform that incorporated paper-based ratiometric sensors and a smartphone. Finally, considerable recoveries were obtained in a range from 96.53 to 103.23% for ultrapure water, tap water, Xiangjiang water and milk with relative standard deviations (RSDs) lower than 9.36% (Table 1 ), indicating satisfactory reliability of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for NOR monitoring in real samples.

In summary, we have developed a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform that utilized Fe3+ quenchers and dual-emission carbon dots for visual quantitative detection of NOR in environmental water samples and milk. Our strategy employed a fluorescence “on–off–on” response mechanism where NOR can activate the Fe3+-quenched emission of BCDs while leaving the red fluorescence of RCDs unchanged. With the gradual addition of NOR, the ratiometric fluorescence probe underwent a color transition from pink to purple to blue, enabling rapid and ultra-sensitive detection of NOR with detection limits as low as 6.84 nM. A portable ratiometric fluorescence sensor platform was constructed by integrating a smartphone with a paper-based detection strategy, on which the concentration-dependent fluorescence color signal in NOR can be converted to B/R value by using its built-in color recognition app. Furthermore, the correlation between B/R value and NOR concentration was mapped to demonstrate the feasibility of NOR analysis in various samples including ultra-pure water, tap water, Xiangjiang River water, and milk. As a result, this established sensor platform provides a new approach for the on-site/real-time visual quantitative detection of NOR in real samples.

Jiaheng Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, and revision. Jun Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, and revision. Feijun Ouyang: Data analysis and discussion. Zhi Zheng: Data analysis and discussion. Xiaowan Huang: Data analysis and discussion. Haitao Zhang: Data analysis and discussion. Dongxiu He: Data analysis and discussion. Suisui He: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, and revision. Hua Wei: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, and revision. Cui-Yun Yu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, and revision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the sponsorship from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22101127), the Hunan Science and Technology Innovation Leading Talent Project (2022RC3080), the Key R & D Program of Hunan province (2021SK2036), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M701646), the Science and Technology Project of Hunan Province (2021RC2084), the Scientific Research Project of Hunan Provincial Health Commission (202213024185 and 202213024688), the Scientific Research Project of Education Department of Hunan Province (22C0204), the Foundation of Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2023JJ40529 and 2023JJ40570), and the National Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program for Undergraduate (202110555065).
